title: liveState always points to the most-recently processed file, making reprocess_live silently operate on the wrong image when a worker handles multiple sequential files.
description: The module-level `liveState` variable is reassigned to the new file's state on every full ORF pipeline run (line 187). If worker W processes file A (liveState = stateA) and then, after being released from the lightbox phase, is dispatched file B (liveState = stateB), any in-flight `reprocess_live` message for taskId A that arrives after B's pipeline starts will call `applyLookToState(liveState, look)` with stateB instead of stateA, rendering the wrong image. The `liveStateMap` was introduced to fix this, but `reprocess_live` still reads `liveState` directly (line 86-87), not `liveStateMap.get(id)`.
evidence: Verified in worker.js line 92: `applyLookToState(liveState, look)` uses the module-level variable unconditionally, ignoring the `id` from the message. Line 187 reassigns `liveState` on every new pipeline run, and `liveStateMap` exists but is never consulted in the `reprocess_live` handler. After a worker processes a second file, all live requests for earlier task IDs silently render the wrong image.

title: liveInFlight flag and livePendingLook state are not coordinated with lightbox navigation, causing stale re-renders.
description: When the user navigates to a different image in the lightbox while a `reprocess_live` round-trip is in flight (`liveInFlight = true`), the live handler at line 402 sets `liveInFlight = false` and then checks `if (lightboxIndex >= 0)` with `msg.id === card._taskId`. If the taskId does not match the new card, the canvas is correctly skipped, but `livePendingLook` is still dispatched to the OLD worker for the OLD taskId (via `triggerLiveUpdate`). This causes a spurious WASM render on the wrong image, wasting CPU. More seriously, `liveInFlight` is reset to false, so the new image's live updates will race with the old image's pending update response.
evidence: Verified at main.js line 403: `liveInFlight = false` fires unconditionally for any live response regardless of which task ID responded. If the user navigated away, the id-mismatch guard (line 406) prevents the canvas draw, but `livePendingLook` at line 417 dispatches a new live request via `triggerLiveUpdate` which uses the current `lightboxIndex` — effectively firing the pending look for the new image while the old image's in-flight slot was just cleared, bypassing the `liveInFlight` gate for the transition window.

title: Phase A (embedded JPEG preview) and Phase B (full WASM pipeline) run concurrently and both write to the same card canvas without synchronization.
description: Phase A calls `drawOrientedThumb` on `card.querySelector('canvas')` when the embedded JPEG is decoded. Phase B (WASM `onThumb`) also calls `drawCanvas` on the same canvas. Because both are async Promise chains, Phase A could draw after Phase B if the embedded JPEG decode is slow but the WASM thumb is fast — leaving a lower-quality thumbnail on screen after the high-quality WASM one was already shown. The guard `card.classList.contains('busy') || card.classList.contains('embedded-thumb')` at line 710 is supposed to prevent this, but Phase B removes 'busy' and 'embedded-thumb' and adds 'encoding' (line 743), so if Phase A resolves after that point, the `classList.contains` check will be false, and the embedded JPEG will NOT overwrite. This sequence is actually correct, but the logic is inverted from the comment intent and relies on non-obvious CSS class state as a mutex.
evidence: The candidate's own analysis concludes the logic is correct: Phase B's `onThumb` (lines 733, 741-742) removes `busy` and `embedded-thumb` and adds `encoding`, so Phase A's guard at line 710 correctly prevents overwriting after Phase B has already drawn the higher-quality WASM thumbnail. The logic is non-obvious but functions correctly — Phase A only draws when `busy` or `embedded-thumb` is set, i.e., only before Phase B has fired. No actual bug exists.

title: handleFileList is declared async but is called without await, discarding its Promise and any thrown errors.
description: `handleFileList` is an `async` function but is called at line 877 from `fileInput`'s `change` event listener (`handleFileList(e.target.files)`) and at line 897 from the `drop` handler without `await`. In the drop handler, the result is awaited inline (`files = await gatherFromItems(...); handleFileList(files)`), making the inner call fire-and-forget. Any rejection inside `handleFileList` (e.g., from a future `await` added inside it) would become an unhandled promise rejection.
evidence: Verified: `handleFileList` is `async` (line 839) but called without `await` at line 877 (sync event handler, cannot await) and line 897 (async drop handler, missing await). Currently `handleFileList` has no internal `await` so rejections don't arise in practice, but the pattern is fragile — any future async addition inside will silently swallow errors as unhandled promise rejections.

title: card._taskId is assigned after pool.submit() returns, creating a window where onThumb could fire before _taskId is set.
description: `pool.submit()` is called inside the `.then()` callback of `file.arrayBuffer()`. The `taskId` returned by `submit` is assigned to `card._taskId` and registered in `cardByTaskId` on the two lines following the submit call. In theory, because JS is single-threaded and the worker runs in a separate thread, the worker cannot post a 'thumb' message that is processed on the main thread before the current microtask checkpoint completes. However, if the WASM pipeline is extremely fast (cached WASM, tiny file), and a microtask flush happens between `submit()` and the `card._taskId = taskId` assignment, the `onThumb` callback would fire with `card._taskId` still `undefined`, and `cardByTaskId.get(taskId)` would return `undefined` for thumb_live updates.
evidence: JavaScript's single-threaded event loop guarantees that a worker's `postMessage` cannot be processed on the main thread until the current task and its microtask queue are fully drained. The `.then()` callback containing both `pool.submit()` and `card._taskId = taskId` (lines 729-811) is a single synchronous execution block — no `await` or microtask yield exists between `submit()` at line 731 and the assignments at lines 810-811. The worker message event cannot fire mid-execution of this callback.

title: Worker message handler falls through to the full ORF pipeline branch for any unknown message type, including future protocol extensions.
description: The message handler checks `type === 'reprocess_live'` and `type === 'reprocess_thumb_live'` explicitly, then falls through to the full pipeline branch which destructures `{ id, bytes, options }`. If a new message type is added in the future (e.g., `type: 'cancel'` or `type: 'ping'`), it will be silently treated as a pipeline task with `bytes = undefined`, causing `process_orf(undefined, ...)` to throw a WASM error. An explicit `else` clause with an unknown-type guard would make the protocol closed.
evidence: Verified in worker.js lines 82-124: the handler has exactly two guarded-return branches for `reprocess_live` and `reprocess_thumb_live`, and then unconditionally destructures `{ id, bytes, options }` from any remaining message. Any future message type (e.g., `cancel`, `ping`) would be destructured with `bytes = undefined`, reach `process_orf(undefined, ...)`, throw inside the try/catch, and post a spurious `error` message back to the main thread.

title: take_rgb() is called once on result, but fullRgb is then passed to both downscale_rgb AND rgb_to_rgba — the buffer may be invalidated after the first WASM call.
description: `take_rgb()` moves the RGB buffer out of WASM memory and returns a `Uint8Array` slice. The generated binding slices from `getUint8ArrayMemory0()` and immediately frees the WASM-side memory. The returned `v1` is a `.slice()` copy (line 178 in the generated JS), so it is a detached JS-owned copy. Both subsequent calls (`downscale_rgb(fullRgb, ...)` and `rgb_to_rgba(fullRgb)`) pass this same `fullRgb` array into WASM via `passArray8ToWasm0`, which copies it into WASM memory each time. This is safe as currently generated, but the pattern depends on `.slice()` behavior in the generated binding — if the binding ever changes to a view instead of a copy, both calls would share WASM memory and the second call after `downscale_rgb` frees the allocation would read freed memory.
evidence: The current code is safe because the generated WASM binding's `take_rgb()` returns a `.slice()` (verified by the finding's reference to line 178 of the generated JS). The multiple-use pattern is valid today. The risk is entirely contingent on wasm-bindgen changing `.slice()` to a view in a future version — a reasonable maintenance concern but not a current bug.

title: wb_mode 0xFFFF sentinel is correctly translated to null, but its meaning as 'absent' is not documented at the use site.
description: The worker correctly handles `result.wb_mode === 0xFFFF ? null : result.wb_mode` (line 179), translating the Rust sentinel to JS null. The main thread's `fmtWb` function (main.js line 1027) uses `exif.wbMode != null` before looking up `OLY_WB_MODE[exif.wbMode]`. This chain is correct. However, the sentinel value 0xFFFF is a magic number defined only in the Rust source; if the Rust side changes the sentinel, the JS null-conversion would silently become a visible numeric value in the UI. The contract should be documented with a constant name.
code_excerpt: wbMode: result.wb_mode === 0xFFFF ? null : result.wb_mode,
evidence: The translation chain (worker.js line 179 → main.js line 1027) is correct and the `fmtWb` function at line 1025-1032 properly guards with `exif.wbMode != null`. The 0xFFFF magic number is a documentation/style concern, not an active bug. No user-visible failure scenario exists with the current code.

title: Rational sentinel 0/0 for exposure and fnumber is correctly guarded by denominator > 0 check in the worker.
description: Lines 170-174 check `result.exposure_den > 0` and `result.fnumber_den > 0` before constructing the rational objects, correctly handling the 0/0 absent sentinel. The main thread's `fmtShutter` and `fmtFNumber` also check `!rat || !rat.d` before dividing. The full chain is correct. One edge case: if `exposure_den` is 0 but `exposure_num` is non-zero (a malformed EXIF value), the rational is discarded entirely rather than reporting an error — this is intentional but could mask corrupt EXIF.
code_excerpt:
exposure:   result.exposure_den   > 0 ? { n: result.exposure_num,   d: result.exposure_den   } : null,
fnumber:    result.fnumber_den    > 0 ? { n: result.fnumber_num,    d: result.fnumber_den    } : null,
focalLength: result.focal_length_den > 0 ? { n: result.focal_length_num, d: result.focal_length_den } : null,
evidence: The finding's own description concludes the chain is correct. Verified at worker.js lines 170-174 and main.js `fmtShutter`/`fmtFNumber` functions (lines 983-994): all guards are present and consistent. The malformed-EXIF edge case (den=0, num≠0) is a documented intentional design choice, not a bug.

title: Pool _onMessage does not handle 'error_live' type for the lightbox — it invokes _liveHandler but the handler at line 402 only checks for 'lightbox_live'.
description: When the worker posts `{ type: 'error_live', error: 'no live state' }`, `_onMessage` routes it to `this._liveHandler(ev.data)`. The live handler at line 402 sets `liveInFlight = false` and then checks `if (msg.type === 'lightbox_live' && ...)`. For an 'error_live' message, this condition is false, the canvas is not updated, `liveInFlight` is correctly reset, and `livePendingLook` is drained. So the state machine recovers correctly, but the error is silently ignored — no console log, no user feedback. Users would see the slider unresponsive without knowing why.
evidence: Verified at main.js lines 302-304: `error_live` is routed to `_liveHandler`. The handler at line 406 only acts on `msg.type === 'lightbox_live'`, so `error_live` silently resets `liveInFlight` with no console.error and no user notification. The state machine recovers, but the slider becomes visually unresponsive (no image update) with no diagnostic output, making the failure invisible to both developers and users.

title: color_matrix_used() is called after take_rgb() — the ProcessResult may be partially consumed, and field access order is undocumented.
description: `result.take_rgb()` moves the RGB buffer out of the Rust struct. The Rust implementation presumably sets the internal Vec to empty after `take_rgb`. Subsequent access to `result.color_matrix_used()` (line 163, called before `take_rgb16_lb` and `take_rgb16_thumb`) relies on the color matrix being a separate field unaffected by the take. This is correct as implemented, but the safe access order is nowhere documented. If Rust refactors the struct to lazily compute color_matrix from the rgb16 buffer, calling it after `take_rgb16_lb` would fail.
code_excerpt:
const fullRgb = result.take_rgb();           // line 149 - moves rgb out
// ...
const colorMatrix = new Float32Array(result.color_matrix_used());  // line 163
const lb16 = result.take_rgb16_lb();         // line 186
const thumb16 = result.take_rgb16_thumb();   // line 192
evidence: This is a purely speculative future-refactoring concern. The color matrix is a separate field in the Rust struct from the pixel buffers, and `color_matrix_used()` is a getter that does not depend on rgb16 state. No current bug exists, and the risk requires a specific hypothetical Rust refactor. Self-reported confidence is 'low'.

title: Worker 'error' event handler only logs to console, leaving the associated task permanently stuck with no user feedback.
description: The pool's worker-level `error` event (line 277: `w.addEventListener('error', (ev) => console.error('worker error:', ev))`) handles uncaught worker exceptions — for example, if the worker script itself throws at the module level or if the Worker process crashes. In this case, no 'error' message is posted through the normal protocol, so `_onMessage` never fires, the task is never deleted, the worker is never released, and the card stays in the 'busy' or 'encoding' state forever. The pool shrinks by one worker permanently.
evidence: Verified at main.js line 277: the `error` event handler only calls `console.error`. On a worker-level crash (script parse error, uncaught synchronous throw, or OOM), the error event fires but `_onMessage` is never called, so any in-progress task record remains in `this.tasks` with the card stuck in `busy`/`encoding` indefinitely. The crashed worker is never removed from `this.workers`, and because it was already popped from `this.free`, the pool's effective capacity shrinks by one permanently.

title: WASM init failure surfaces as a caught error in the pipeline try/catch, but the wasmReady promise is cached in a rejected state and all subsequent calls will also fail.
description: `ensureWasm()` assigns `wasmReady = init()` once. If `init()` rejects (network failure fetching the .wasm, invalid module, etc.), `wasmReady` holds the rejected promise permanently. Every subsequent call to `ensureWasm()` awaits that same rejected promise, so every new file task dispatched to this worker will immediately throw, posting an 'error' back. The worker remains alive but useless. There is no retry logic and no signal to the main thread that this worker should be replaced.
evidence: Verified at worker.js lines 41-44: `wasmReady` is assigned `init()` exactly once and never cleared. A rejected `init()` promise is permanently cached. Every subsequent `await ensureWasm()` immediately rejects, the catch block at line 227 posts `{ type: 'error' }`, and the pool slot is freed (via `_releaseWorker` in the error path) — but the worker continues to accept and immediately fail all future tasks. On a flaky network, all POOL_SIZE workers could end up in this permanent-failure state with no retry mechanism.

title: reprocess_thumb_live silently swallows errors per taskId with an empty catch, providing no signal for systematic failures.
description: In the `reprocess_thumb_live` handler, errors from `applyLookToState` are caught with an empty `catch (err) {}`. If `apply_look` in WASM throws (e.g., buffer size mismatch, corrupted state), the gallery thumbnail silently stops updating. The main thread has no way to detect this condition. A degraded-mode indicator or at minimum a `console.error` would improve debuggability.
evidence: Verified at worker.js lines 110-119: the empty `catch (err) {}` at line 116 is present and swallows all errors from `applyLookToState`. If WASM throws for any taskId (e.g., corrupt rgb16 state, buffer size mismatch after re-process), that thumbnail permanently stops updating on slider changes with no console output and no signal to the main thread. A `console.error` at minimum is needed.

title: Object URL created for the JXL download link is never revoked when the card is re-processed, causing a memory leak.
description: In `onDone`, `URL.createObjectURL(blob)` creates a persistent object URL assigned to `link.href`. When `startConvert` is called again on the same card (re-process), the link's `href` is cleared (`link.removeAttribute('href')`) but `URL.revokeObjectURL` is never called on the old URL. Each re-process of the same file leaks a Blob-backed URL that holds the full JXL bytes in memory for the lifetime of the page.
evidence: Verified at main.js lines 757-762: `URL.createObjectURL(blob)` assigns a new URL to `link.href` on every `onDone` call. The re-process path in `startConvert` (lines 675-678) calls `link.removeAttribute('href')` but there is no `URL.revokeObjectURL` call anywhere before the new URL is assigned. Each re-process of the same file leaks a JXL-sized Blob URL for the page lifetime. Searched the full file and confirmed no `revokeObjectURL` is called on the download link href.

title: JXL blob URL used for the inline download link has no revocation on lightbox close or page unload.
description: The lightbox download button (`lbDownloadBtn`) creates a Blob URL via `URL.createObjectURL` inside a `toBlob` callback and schedules `revokeObjectURL` after 30 seconds via `setTimeout`. This is a reasonable pattern, but `toBlob` is called on the lightbox canvas which is the RGB8 render, not the JXL. If the user clicks the download button many times in rapid succession, many short-lived Blob URLs accumulate until each 30s timer fires.
evidence: Verified at main.js lines 1133-1143: the 30-second `setTimeout(revokeObjectURL)` is present and will eventually clean up each URL. The concern (multiple rapid clicks accumulating blobs) is real but requires deliberate abuse; in normal use, one click triggers one download and the URL is revoked 30 seconds later. The fix (revoke immediately after `.click()`) would be an improvement but no significant leak exists under normal usage.

title: closeLightbox does not cancel in-flight liveInFlight state, so a pending reprocess_live response will still update the canvas after close.
description: When the lightbox is closed, `liveInFlight` is not reset to false and `livePendingLook` is not cleared. If a `reprocess_live` round-trip is in flight when the user closes the lightbox, the live handler will fire after close, set `liveInFlight = false`, and (if `livePendingLook` was set) trigger `triggerLiveUpdate` against `lightboxIndex = -1`, which `scheduleLiveUpdate` guards against. The canvas write is also guarded by `lightboxIndex >= 0`. So the visible effect is benign, but the unnecessary WASM compute still runs and the `liveInFlight` flag stays stuck as true until the response arrives.
evidence: Verified the guard chain: `triggerLiveUpdate` at line 395-400 immediately returns if `cards[lightboxIndex]` is undefined (which it is when `lightboxIndex = -1`). The `livePendingLook` path at line 417-420 calls `triggerLiveUpdate(pending)` which exits at line 397 because `card` is undefined. No WASM compute is triggered after close. The description's claim that 'unnecessary WASM compute still runs' is incorrect — the guards prevent it. The only real issue is the in-flight response's canvas write, which is also guarded by `lightboxIndex >= 0` at line 404.

title: reprocessLive finds worker by _lastTaskId but a worker can hold multiple task IDs, causing incorrect targeting.
description: After the worker is released post-lightbox, the pool sets `w._lastTaskId = id` on each release, overwriting the previous value. If the user opens lightbox for image A (taskId=1) and then image B (taskId=2) is dispatched to the same worker, `_lastTaskId` becomes 2. Calling `reprocessLive` for taskId=1 would fail silently (returns false) even though taskId=1's liveState still lives in that worker's `liveStateMap`. The intent was that `_taskIds` (a Set) would be the authoritative source, but `reprocessLive` only looks at `_lastTaskId`.
evidence: Verified at main.js lines 335-344: `_releaseWorker` sets `worker._lastTaskId = id` (line 338) and also adds to `worker._taskIds` Set (line 340). But `reprocessLive` at line 356 searches only by `_lastTaskId`. Once a worker processes a second file, its `_lastTaskId` is overwritten with the newer task ID, making live re-render for any earlier task silently fail — `reprocessLive` returns false and `triggerLiveUpdate` at line 398 returns early without updating the lightbox.

title: _releaseWorker unconditionally pushes the worker to free[] even if it is already there, risking double-dispatch.
description: When a 'lightbox' message arrives, `_releaseWorker` is called to free the slot. If an 'error' message subsequently arrives for the same task (line 328: `if (!t.released) this._releaseWorker(worker, id)`), the guard `t.released` prevents a second push. However, the `_release` helper (line 347) also calls `_releaseWorker` without checking `t.released`, creating a path where a caller of `_release` on an already-released task would push the worker twice. Currently `_release` is only called for early errors before lightbox, but the guard is subtly inconsistent.
evidence: Verified: `_release` is defined at lines 347-350 but has no call sites in the current main.js — it is an unused method. The double-push risk exists only if `_release` is called on an already-released task, which cannot happen with the current code. The inconsistency between `_release` (no `t.released` guard) and the error handler (has guard) is a maintenance hazard worth noting but is not an active bug.

title: nextInLightbox wraps index modulo cards.length but cards[] can be empty, producing NaN lightboxIndex.
description: `nextInLightbox` computes `(lightboxIndex + dir + cards.length) % cards.length`. If `cards.length === 0`, this yields `NaN` (division by zero in modulo). While `lightboxIndex < 0` guard fires before cards can be empty in practice (the lightbox cannot be opened on an empty grid), a defensive `cards.length === 0` check would make the invariant explicit and safe against future refactors.
evidence: The lightbox can only be opened via `openLightbox(card)` which is triggered by clicking a card element (line 489). `cards` is populated before any card element exists. The `lightboxIndex < 0` guard at line 1116 is always true when `cards` is empty because no card click can set it otherwise. The NaN scenario is structurally unreachable with the current code.

title: contrast-boost adds 0.15 to the normalized contrast value, potentially exceeding the documented [-1, +1] range.
description: The contrast slider emits values in [-100, 100] which are divided by 100 to get [-1, +1]. When `contrastBoostEl` is checked, 0.15 is added to the already-normalised value. If the contrast slider is at +100 (normalized 1.0), the total sent to `apply_look` is 1.15, outside the API's stated normalised range. The WASM `apply_look` may clamp internally, but the out-of-range value is undocumented as safe.
evidence: Verified at main.js line 140: `lookValueFor('contrast')` returns `raw / 100` where `raw` is in [-100, 100], giving a range of [-1, +1]. Adding 0.15 when the boost checkbox is checked pushes the maximum to 1.15. The WASM `apply_look` function receives this value with no JS-side clamping. Whether WASM clamps internally is not visible in this file. The out-of-range value is a real contract violation; at minimum it should be clamped to 1.0 in JS before dispatch.

title: handleFileList has no deduplication — the same ORF file dropped twice creates two separate cards.
description: `handleFileList` filters by extension and calls `startConvert` for every entry. There is no check against already-loaded filenames or File identity. Dropping the same folder twice will create duplicate cards, duplicate tasks, and duplicate stats entries. The cards array grows unboundedly, and `lightboxIndex` into it can refer to duplicates.
evidence: Verified at main.js lines 839-842: no filename, File object identity, or any other deduplication check exists. Dropping the same folder twice calls `startConvert(f)` for every ORF, creating duplicate cards in the `cards` array and duplicate entries in `cardByTaskId`. Workers process the same bytes twice wasting CPU/memory, and the UI shows duplicate thumbnails.

title: tasks.delete(id) on 'done' before the worker is definitively freed can leave orphan tasks if lightbox never arrived.
description: The comment at line 317 says the worker is freed on lightbox, but in the 'done' handler (line 322-324) the code calls `tasks.delete(id)` without confirming the worker was already released. If WASM somehow emits 'done' without a prior 'lightbox' (e.g., a future code path skipping lightbox for small images), the worker would never be returned to the free pool — it would be permanently lost. The `t.released` flag is never checked in the 'done' branch.
evidence: Verified at main.js lines 322-324: the comment 'Worker already freed on lightbox' documents the design assumption. The worker protocol in worker.js always emits `lightbox` before `done` (lines 204-226 show `lightbox` is posted synchronously before the `await encode_jxl` that leads to `done`). The scenario requires worker.js to be modified to skip `lightbox`, which doesn't exist today. A defensive check (`if (!t.released) this._releaseWorker(worker, id)`) would be good practice but addresses only a hypothetical future change.

title: applyLookBtn selects all cards then calls reprocessSelected, but selection state is not restored afterward.
description: When no cards are selected, the 'Apply Look' button selects all cards (`c.classList.add('selected')`) and then calls `reprocessSelected`. After the reprocess completes, all cards remain permanently in the selected state. The UI checkmarks will all show '✓' until the user manually deselects. This is an implicit side-effect on UI state that users will find surprising.
evidence: Verified at main.js lines 186-196: when no cards are selected (line 191 `!selected.length`), the handler adds `selected` class to all cards (line 193) without updating the `.thumb-select` text content to '✓' (that update happens in the click handler at line 473-474, not here). After `reprocessSelected()` completes, all cards have `selected` class but their thumb-select buttons still show '·', creating an inconsistent visual state. More critically, subsequent slider changes will trigger gallery live updates for all cards unexpectedly.

title: RGB-to-RGBA conversion (3-channel to 4-channel expansion) runs on the main thread inside drawCanvas, blocking the UI for large images.
description: The `drawCanvas` function runs a tight loop converting RGB to RGBA on the main thread: `for (let i = 0, j = 0; i < rgb.length; i += 3, j += 4)`. For a 1800px lightbox image (~9.7M pixels), this loop touches ~29 MB of source data with 9.7M iterations. This runs synchronously on the main thread every time a lightbox image is displayed or a live update fires. The same work is already done in WASM via `rgb_to_rgba` in the worker for the JXL encode — a parallel path should be used here. In the live path (line 409-414), the same loop runs on every slider move, causing jank.
evidence: Verified at main.js lines 519-531: the RGB→RGBA loop runs synchronously on the main thread. For a 1800-pixel-long-edge lightbox image (approximately 1800×1200 = 2.16M pixels), the loop has 2.16M iterations touching 6.48MB of source data. This blocks the event loop for an estimated 5-20ms per call depending on hardware. The same function is invoked from the live handler (line 446) and `drawLightboxForCard` (line 943), making it a repeated jank source on every slider change and lightbox open.

title: Live lightbox update also performs the RGB-to-RGBA expansion on the main thread inline in the message handler.
description: The `liveHandler` callback executes `for (let i = 0, j = 0; i < msg.rgb.length; i += 3, j += 4)` directly in the message handler, which runs on the main thread. Since `msg.rgb` for the lightbox can be up to 1800px on the long edge (~9.7 MP), this expansion blocks the event loop for tens of milliseconds per slider change. The worker could send RGBA directly (at the cost of 33% more IPC data) or the worker could call `rgb_to_rgba` before posting the live message, keeping the main thread free.
evidence: Verified at main.js lines 409-413: an inline RGB→RGBA loop runs in the live handler on the main thread on every slider debounce response. This is a separate instance of the same loop as in `drawCanvas` and runs for every live update. Since this is in the critical path for slider responsiveness, it directly causes perceived jank. The fix (have the worker send RGBA via `rgb_to_rgba`) is straightforward since `rgb_to_rgba` is already imported in worker.js.

title: thumbRgb and bigRgb are sent as transferable buffers, but fullRgb is retained in the worker for rgb_to_rgba — causing an extra full-res copy.
description: `fullRgb` (full resolution RGB8, potentially 60+ MB for a 20 MP sensor) is kept alive in the worker to be passed to `rgb_to_rgba` for JXL encoding. Meanwhile `thumbRgb.buffer` and `bigRgb.buffer` are transferred. The `downscale_rgb` call at line 196 copies `fullRgb` into WASM memory, returns a new JS Uint8Array, and then frees the WASM copy — but `fullRgb` itself remains valid in JS. After `bigRgb` is posted, `fullRgb` (~60MB) stays alive until `rgb_to_rgba(fullRgb)` at line 212. If JXL encoding is slow (seconds), this large buffer is held in the worker's heap for that entire duration, preventing GC. Passing the RGBA-converted version instead and freeing `fullRgb` earlier would reduce peak worker memory.
evidence: Verified at worker.js lines 149-213: `fullRgb` from `take_rgb()` is used at lines 196, 205, and 212. The JXL encode at line 215 (`await encode_jxl(imageData, ...)`) is an async operation that yields, and `fullRgb` plus the RGBA copy both remain live until `encode_jxl` completes. For a 20 MP image at 3 bytes/px, `fullRgb` is ~60MB and `rgba` is ~80MB — approximately 140MB of peak allocation held during potentially multi-second JXL encoding.

title: triggerGalleryLiveUpdate calls cards.filter() and c._taskId lookups on every debounced slider event.
description: `triggerGalleryLiveUpdate` iterates the full `cards` array to find selected cards with `_taskId` on every gallery live update. For large batches (hundreds of files), this is an O(n) scan per slider event firing at ~12 Hz (80ms debounce). The `cardByTaskId` Map already provides O(1) lookups by taskId, but the inverse direction (card → selected?) requires a scan. Maintaining a `selectedCards` Set that is updated on select/deselect toggles would reduce this to O(selected) instead of O(total).
evidence: Verified at main.js lines 437-442: `cards.filter(c => c.classList.contains('selected') && c._taskId)` scans the full array on every debounced slider change. `classList.contains` performs a DOMTokenList lookup per card. At 80ms debounce with 100+ files, this is ~12.5 scans/second over a potentially large array. Confirmed as a real O(n) scan per event, though impact is negligible below ~50 cards.

title: card.querySelector() is called multiple times in the onThumb/onDone callbacks for each file, inside async closures.
description: The `onThumb` and `onDone` message handlers each call `card.querySelector('canvas')`, `card.querySelector('.thumb-dl-btn')`, `card.querySelector('.download')`, `card.querySelector('.size')`, `card.querySelector('.time')` separately. These are O(subtree) DOM traversals. For a batch of 100 files completing simultaneously, this is 500+ querySelector calls in a tight callback loop. Caching the element references at `makeCard` time (storing them on the card element or in a closure) would eliminate repeated traversals.
evidence: Verified at main.js lines 733-769: `card.querySelector` is called 2 times in `onThumb` (lines 733, 740) and 4 times in `onDone` (lines 759, 764, 767). These are separate DOM subtree traversals per file per callback. Each card's subtree is small (~6 elements), so individual cost is low, but for a 100-file batch all completing near-simultaneously, the pattern generates hundreds of traversals. Caching references at `makeCard` time is the idiomatic fix.

title: canvas.width and canvas.height are reassigned on every drawCanvas call, clearing the canvas and forcing a layout recalculation even when dimensions have not changed.
description: Setting `canvas.width = w` unconditionally clears the canvas and triggers a style recalculation in the browser, even if the dimensions are identical to the current ones. For the gallery thumb live updates (which fire repeatedly on slider changes), the thumb canvas dimensions are constant after the initial render. A guard `if (canvas.width !== w || canvas.height !== h)` before reassigning would avoid the unnecessary clear-and-restyle cycle in the steady state.
evidence: Verified at main.js lines 520-521: `canvas.width = w` and `canvas.height = h` are set unconditionally on every call. Assigning canvas dimensions (even to the same values) is specified to clear the bitmap and can trigger layout. For gallery thumb live updates via `pool.setThumbLiveHandler` at line 446 (which calls `drawCanvas`), the dimensions are constant after initial render, so every slider-change live update unnecessarily clears and re-layouts all selected thumbnails.

title: rgb_to_rgba result is wrapped in Uint8ClampedArray by copy — a zero-copy path using the buffer directly would save an allocation.
description: `rgb_to_rgba(fullRgb)` returns a `Uint8Array` (from WASM-generated `.slice()`). It is then wrapped with `new Uint8ClampedArray(rgba)`, which copies the underlying buffer. `Uint8ClampedArray` and `Uint8Array` share the same underlying memory representation; the copy can be avoided by using `new Uint8ClampedArray(rgba.buffer, rgba.byteOffset, rgba.byteLength)`. For a 20 MP image, this avoids a ~80 MB allocation and copy before JXL encoding.
code_excerpt:
const rgba = rgb_to_rgba(fullRgb);
const imageData = { data: new Uint8ClampedArray(rgba), width: w, height: h };
evidence: Verified at worker.js lines 212-213: `new Uint8ClampedArray(rgba)` where `rgba` is a `Uint8Array` uses the ArrayBuffer-copy constructor path, allocating a new backing buffer and copying all bytes. The zero-copy alternative `new Uint8ClampedArray(rgba.buffer, rgba.byteOffset, rgba.byteLength)` is a one-line fix that avoids an ~80MB allocation and memcpy for a 20 MP image. The WASM-generated `.slice()` already gives a JS-owned buffer so the view would be valid.

title: lightboxInfo.innerHTML = '' is safe on its own but adjacent code builds DOM via innerHTML in makeCard, mixing patterns.
description: In `renderInfoPanel`, all EXIF values (camera make/model, lens, datetime, GPS, etc.) are inserted via `dd.textContent = value`, which is XSS-safe. However `makeCard` at line 456 uses `card.innerHTML = ...` with a static template literal containing no user data, which is also safe. The pattern mix (innerHTML for structure, textContent for data) is correct but fragile — a future developer adding EXIF data to the makeCard template string could introduce XSS. No active vulnerability exists.
evidence: Verified at main.js lines 457-467 and 1058-1089: the `innerHTML` in `makeCard` uses a fully static template literal with no variable interpolation. All dynamic user data (EXIF values, filenames) is assigned via `textContent` (line 468, lines 1083-1084). No active XSS vulnerability exists; this is a style/future-proofing concern only.

title: Error message from WASM is stored in card.dataset.error without sanitization, but dataset assignment is XSS-safe.
description: The `onError` handler assigns `msg.error` (a string from the worker) to `card.dataset.error`. HTMLElement dataset sets are reflected as `data-*` attribute values; the browser HTML-encodes them when serialized, so this is not a direct XSS vector. However if any other code path reads `card.dataset.error` and inserts it via innerHTML, that would be unsafe. No such path exists currently, but the pattern should be documented.
evidence: Verified at main.js lines 800-806 and searched the full file for `dataset.error` usage: it is only assigned here and never read back into innerHTML or any other injection-capable sink. Dataset assignment is browser-HTML-encoded. No XSS path exists.

title: File type filtering uses only a filename extension check, not MIME type or magic-byte validation.
description: The `isOrf` function checks `/\.orf$/i.test(file.name)`. A user can rename any file to `.orf` and submit it, which will then be read entirely into memory via `file.arrayBuffer()` and forwarded to the WASM pipeline. The WASM decoder will likely reject it with an error, but the full file bytes are loaded unconditionally. A 4 GB file named `bomb.orf` would exhaust browser memory before WASM even sees it. A size limit check (`file.size > MAX_BYTES`) would mitigate this.
evidence: Verified at main.js lines 844-846: only a regex extension check is performed. The `file.arrayBuffer()` call at line 728 loads the entire file unconditionally. No size limit is applied before or after the extension check. A locally crafted 2-4GB file with a `.orf` extension would be read into memory in its entirety before WASM processes it, potentially causing an OOM crash in the browser tab. A `file.size > MAX_BYTES` guard is the minimal fix.

title: Preset look values are persisted to localStorage and round-tripped through JSON.parse without schema validation.
description: Preset data is read with `JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem(...))` and the resulting object's `look` fields are used directly as numeric slider values via `applyLookValues`. If a user or extension tampers with localStorage to insert non-numeric values (e.g., strings, null, objects), these are passed to `lookValueFor` which calls `Number(el.value)` — likely producing NaN rather than a security breach. No code injection is possible since the data goes to input element `.value` and number coercion, not to innerHTML or eval.
evidence: Verified at main.js lines 210-213 and `applyLookValues` at lines 219-228: preset look values are applied via `el.value = v` (input element value assignment) and read back via `Number(el.value)`. Tampered non-numeric values produce NaN which propagates to WASM as 0 (since NaN in Rust float params is handled by the `??` defaults in worker.js). No code injection vector exists — data never reaches innerHTML, eval, or any similar sink.

title: Worker message handler in _onMessage does not validate the message origin or structure before acting on it.
description: Workers created with `new Worker(new URL('./worker.js', import.meta.url), {type:'module'})` are same-origin, so rogue postMessage injection from external origins is not possible. However there is no `instanceof` or type-narrowing check on `ev.data` fields before destructuring `{ id, type }`. If the worker ever emits a malformed message (e.g., during error recovery), accessing `.type` on undefined would throw. This is low risk in practice but `ev.data?.type` would be safer.
evidence: Workers created via `new Worker(url, {type:'module'})` can only receive messages posted by their own script and the main thread — no external origin injection is possible. `ev.data` in a Worker message event is always the value passed to `postMessage()`, which in worker.js is always a plain object with `id` and `type` fields. The `ev.data` being null/undefined would require `self.postMessage(null)` in worker.js, which never occurs. This is an extremely defensive concern with no realistic failure scenario.
